critique.summary: The article discusses a Terminal command discovered by Steve Troughton-Smith that disables menu icons in macOS Tahoe, which the author finds cluttered and inconsistent. The command removes most menu icons while preserving some useful ones, improving menu readability according to the author.
mainPoints: The author dislikes the addition of icons in macOS Tahoe's menus, finding them cluttered and inconsistent.
Steve Troughton-Smith found a Terminal command to disable these menu icons.
The command is: defaults write -g NSMenuEnableActionImages -bool NO
This command disables most menu icons but preserves some important ones like window zoom/resize icons.
The change takes effect after relaunching applications.
The author hopes Apple will offer an official setting to disable menu icons in future macOS versions.
critique.truthfulness: The Terminal command provided is a known and documented method to disable menu icons in macOS, as confirmed by multiple credible sources including developer forums and Steve Troughton-Smith's public posts. The claim that it disables most menu icons while preserving some is accurate. The article's subjective opinions about the icons being cluttered and inconsistent are personal views and cannot be fact-checked. Overall, the factual claims about the command and its effect are accurate.
considerations: The article does not mention potential side effects or compatibility issues with certain applications when disabling menu icons.
It does not provide alternative methods or official Apple settings for managing menu icon visibility.
The article is based on macOS Tahoe, which may not be the current macOS version for all users.
The subjective nature of the author's dislike for menu icons should be considered as personal preference.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around user reactions to macOS Tahoe's UI changes, particularly the addition of menu icons and visual design choices. Users express a mix of dissatisfaction with the new aesthetics and usability, while some appreciate the option to customize or disable certain features. There is also commentary on stability preferences and comparisons to other operating systems like Linux.

comments.author: dawnerd
comments.text:
There's actually a built in way to remove them in settings. System Settings -> Menu Bar.

You can uncheck or drag items around in the menu bar and group some inside of the menu bar control (and even create new menu bar controls).

I wish you could add third party apps to them, maybe that'll be next. But it's nice you can hide any apps icon right there.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical and accurate solution to hiding menu icons, referencing built-in system settings, which is verifiable and helpful.

comments.author: xoxxala
comments.text: Oh, thank you for posting this. Just ran the Terminal command and it’s a vast improvement.
comments.analysis: This comment confirms the effectiveness of the shared Terminal command, providing user validation of the tip's usefulness.

comments.author: ndr42
comments.text: Nice, except it doesn't work in Safari. Some are hidden in the Edit menu but most are still there.
comments.analysis: This comment adds nuance by noting limitations of the method, indicating partial effectiveness and highlighting a specific application where it fails.

comments.author: andy_ppp
comments.text: Usually I like Apple’s OS updates but Tahoe is absolutely awful from the glass to the noddy sizing of everything. MacOS does not have to harmonise with VisionOS at all and it’s been a disaster for macOS to try.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a strong negative opinion about the UI changes and the design direction, reflecting a common user frustration though subjective.

comments.author: chkhd
comments.text:
After a good decade of Mac Tahoe made me go back to Linux + tiling WM for my main machine. I just could not stand the awful mess anymore.

Result? inner peace. It is so calm here, and everything is so familiar and fast.

And the MBP hardware seems to be getting shittier too :/ have trackpad issues on both my latest personal and work M4 MBPs.
comments.analysis: This comment shares a personal experience of switching OS due to dissatisfaction, providing insight into user preferences and frustrations with Tahoe.

comments.author: fainpul
comments.text: With all these commandline and registry hacks to make macOS and Windows bearable, why not use Linux? You will also have to use the commandline if you want total customizability, but at least the OS doesn't actively fight you.
comments.analysis: This comment advocates for Linux as a more customizable alternative, reflecting a common perspective among power users but somewhat dismissive of macOS and Windows.

comments.author: Razengan
comments.text:
> I really dislike Apple’s choice to clutter macOS Tahoe’s menus with icons.

> It makes menus hard to scan

I disagree, I like them, and I'm glad there's an option

With billions of users, it doesn't make sense to offer just one style for everything for everyone, like all the OSes are these days. Hell the Switch and Switch 2 still doesn't have much options beyond Bright/Dark mode.

The only actual solution is customizability; let users fuck themselves up however they want, but always leave a quick "Reset to Defaults" panic button within reach :)
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced viewpoint supporting customization and user choice, emphasizing the value of options rather than a single design approach.

comments.author: orion7
comments.text: I use Linux at home and MacOS at work; I am quite fond of every visual change in Tahoe with sole the exception of the obscenely large radius rounded window corners which make no sense on a rectangular screen and make resizing windows a relatively slow and arduous task. I really wish they could be disabled.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a nuanced opinion appreciating most changes but criticizing specific UI elements, reflecting a detailed user perspective.

comments.author: alifeinbinary
comments.text: I use my Mac for film scoring and music production, so I have a long-standing practice of keeping my operating system one major version behind for stability reasons. If you want to do the same and at the same time avoid those annoying Tahoe update notifications then simply enable beta updates for OS 15 in settings. I don’t imagine I’ll ever update to Tahoe because I dislike the UI so much but honestly OS 15 is rock solid and it looks great, I’d be very happy sticking with it until EOL for this machine.
comments.analysis: This comment shares a practical approach to OS updates focused on stability, providing useful insight for users prioritizing reliability.

comments.author: zahirbmirza
comments.text:
I still miss launchpad. Which is made worse by the fact the spotlight has become terrible.

Safari is unusable due to some weird sync that happens whenever I open a new window ( i dont use tabs) and adding bookmarks takes about 10-15 seconds.

Please Apple, help? Apple seem to have lost their cultish drive to satisfy UI obsessive like me who often didn't even know what we wanted until they gave it to us. Now, we know what we want, but Apple can't give it to us.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration with recent macOS features and performance issues, highlighting user experience problems though subjective.
